seo.title: AI Agent Observability in Azure AI Agent Service Agents
seo.description: How to detect and fix ai agent observability in Azure AI Agent Service agents. Framework-specific detection, fixes, and Vex automation.

content.intro: In Azure AI Agent Service, AI Agent Observability challenges arise from opaque tool integrations, grounding data pipelines, and thread management that lack built-in validation against enterprise policies. Agents often fail silently when Bing Search returns outdated information, file search tools expose Azure metadata instead of content, or token limits are exceeded without graceful degradation. Vex provides middleware observability to surface these issues before they impact enterprise workflows.

howItManifests.scenario: Tool execution results not validated against enterprise policies
howItManifests.symptom: Agent returns PII-containing responses from unfiltered tools
howItManifests.impact: Data leakage violations trigger compliance alerts

howItManifests.scenario: Grounding data from Bing Search containing outdated information
howItManifests.symptom: Agent cites 2023 pricing data in 2025 responses
howItManifests.impact: Customer decisions based on stale enterprise information

howItManifests.scenario: File search returning Azure-specific metadata instead of content
howItManifests.symptom: Responses contain blob URLs and SAS tokens instead of document text
howItManifests.impact: Users receive unusable links requiring additional permissions

howItManifests.scenario: Agent thread token limits exceeded without graceful degradation
howItManifests.symptom: Thread creation fails with 429 errors mid-conversation
howItManifests.impact: Lost conversation context forces users to restart interactions

detection.name: Bing Grounding Staleness Check
detection.description: Validates Bing search results against publication dates and freshness thresholds

detection.name: Token Limit Monitoring
detection.description: Tracks Azure AI Agent thread token usage against limits with alerts
detection.code:
from azure.ai.projects.types import ThreadMetrics

def check_thread_token_limits(thread_metrics: ThreadMetrics, warning_threshold: float = 0.9) -> str:
    total_tokens = thread_metrics.input_tokens + thread_metrics.output_tokens
    max_tokens = 128000  # Azure AI Agent Service GPT-4o limit
    
    usage_pct = total_tokens / max_tokens
    if usage_pct > 1.0:
        return '🚨 EXCEEDED'
    elif usage_pct > warning_threshold:
        return f'⚠️  {usage_pct:.1%} - Approaching limit'
    return f'✅ {usage_pct:.1%}'

# Monitor active threads
for thread in client.agents.list_threads(project_name, agent_id):
    metrics = client.agents.get_thread_metrics(thread.id)
    status = check_thread_token_limits(metrics)
    print(f'Thread {thread.id}: {status} ({metrics.input_tokens + metrics.output_tokens}/128k)')

fixes.name: Graceful Token Limit Handler
fixes.description: Implements thread summarization before token exhaustion
fixes.code:
class TokenManager(VexMiddleware):
    def __init__(self):
        self.summarization_threshold = 0.95

    async def before_thread_step(self, thread):
        metrics = thread.metrics
        if self._needs_summarization(metrics):
            summary = await self.summarize_thread(thread)
            await self.create_summary_thread(summary, thread.id)
            await self.log('Created summary thread to prevent token exhaustion')

    def _needs_summarization(self, metrics):
        total = metrics.input_tokens + metrics.output_tokens
        return total / 128000 > self.summarization_threshold

# Deploy to Azure AI Agent Service
token_mgr = TokenManager()
client.agents.assign_middleware(agent_id, token_mgr, 'pre_step')

vexAutomation.heading: Automating AI Agent Observability Detection with Vex
vexAutomation.description:
Vex integrates as middleware directly into Azure AI Agent Service processing pipelines, automatically detecting all four common observability failure modes in real-time. The Vex SDK hooks into agent thread events, tool calls, and grounding pipelines to validate outputs against enterprise policies, freshness requirements, and token limits before issues reach users.

Vex provides automated remediation through configurable rulesets - blocking PII, routing stale data, and creating summary threads - all without code changes to existing agents. Dashboards show failure rates, compliance violations, and token usage trends across all Azure AI Agents.

Deployment takes minutes: `pip install azure-ai-projects vex-sdk`, then register Vex middleware with a single API call. Vex handles the observability complexity while preserving Azure's enterprise security controls.
vexAutomation.code:
from vex_sdk import VexAgentObserver
from azure.ai.projects.client import ProjectsClient

# Install: pip install azure-ai-projects vex-sdk

client = ProjectsClient.from_azure_cli()

# Auto-configure Vex for all observability failure modes
observer = VexAgentObserver(
    azure_client=client,
    rules={
        'pii_policy': {'enabled': True, 'block': True},
        'bing_freshness': {'max_age_days': 30},
        'file_content': {'strip_metadata': True},
        'token_limits': {'summarize_at': 0.95}
    }
)

# Single command deployment
observer.deploy_to_project(
    project_name='my-enterprise-agents',
    agent_ids=['agent-123', 'agent-456']
)

# Real-time monitoring dashboard
observer.start_dashboard(port=8080)
print('🚀 Vex Observability active - Dashboard: http://localhost:8080')

# Automated alerts
@observer.on_violation
def handle_policy_violation(violation):
    print(f'🚨 {violation.type}: {violation.details}')
    # Send to Azure Monitor / Teams
